read.heading: Unit 1 review
paragraphs: You've covered a lot of ground in this unit: what makes something alive, the two basic kinds of cells, the membrane that gives a cell its identity, and a first look inside the eukaryotic cell. This review pulls together questions from across all four lessons.
If anything feels uncertain, that's completely normal, this is exactly what review is for. Take your time, and if you get stuck, the earlier lessons are always there to revisit.

questions.text: Every living thing is made of at least one cell.
questions.feedback_correct: Correct. This is the foundation of cell theory, no exceptions among known life.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Actually, this is true. Every known living organism is made of one or more cells.

questions.feedback_correct: Right. Prokaryotic cells have no nucleus at all, that's the key difference from eukaryotic cells.
questions.feedback_incorrect: This one's false. Prokaryotic cells have no nucleus, their genetic material floats freely inside the cell.

questions.sentence_before: The cell membrane is made of a double layer of molecules called the
questions.feedback_correct: Correct. The phospholipid bilayer forms the structural barrier of every cell membrane.
questions.feedback_incorrect: The correct word is phospholipid. This double layer of molecules forms the structural basis of the cell membrane.

questions.sentence_before: The cell's power plants, which convert nutrients into usable energy, are called
questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Mitochondria handle the energy conversion that powers the rest of the cell.
questions.feedback_incorrect: The correct term is mitochondria. These organelles convert nutrients into the energy the cell relies on.

questions.text: Match each structure to its role.

pairs.right: Controls what enters and exits the cell
Builds and processes proteins and fats

questions.feedback_correct: All correct. That's the full picture from this unit, you're ready to move on to Unit 2.
questions.feedback_incorrect: A couple of these didn't line up. Take another look back through the unit's lessons before retrying.

questions.text: Bacteria are an example of a eukaryotic organism.
questions.feedback_correct: Right. Bacteria are prokaryotic. Eukaryotic organisms include plants, animals, and fungi.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Not quite. Bacteria are prokaryotic, not eukaryotic.
